what_manipulated: proportion of congruent vs incongruent stimulus presentations for repeated items
what_measured: reaction time difference between congruent and incongruent trials (Stroop effect magnitude)
experiment: Units (word presentations) were varied in the proportion of congruent vs incongruent color matchings. The comparison was between words presented mostly congruent versus mostly incongruent. Analysis examined whether response predictions based on stimulus identity (contingency) or attentional modulation better explained reaction time patterns. Reanalysis of prior data and new experiments tested these competing mechanisms.

what_manipulated: sampling strategy (natural vs deliberate) when gathering information before making inference decisions
what_measured: accuracy of inferences about latent properties of a population based on sample data
experiment: Units (information samples drawn by participants) were generated under two search strategies with different statistical properties. The comparison was between inferences made after natural sampling (sample reflects population composition) versus deliberate sampling (sample optimized for a specific inference). Analysis examined the accuracy of inferences about actual superiority and distributional origins.

what_manipulated: location match between study presentation and test probe in a recognition memory task
what_measured: accuracy and speed of rejecting probes that match in content but differ in location
experiment: Units (probe judgments) were varied by whether the probe matched a list item at its original location or appeared in a different location. The comparison was serial position curves for correctly recognized items versus intrusion probes (items from wrong locations). Analysis examined whether dual-process theory (familiarity and recollection via two retrieval routes) explained the parallel curves across conditions.

what_manipulated: strength of memory representations via spaced presentations, processing time, or processing depth
what_measured: magnitude of directed forgetting effect (performance difference between remember and forget instructions)
experiment: Units (strengthened items) were created through operations hypothesized to enhance context strength (spaced presentations) or item strength without context change (processing time and depth). The comparison was forgetting of strong versus weak items under directed forgetting instructions. Analysis tested whether context or item strength mediated directed forgetting.

what_manipulated: language experience group membership (monolingual vs bilingual)
what_measured: efficiency and patterns of inhibition across three different inhibition tasks
experiment: Units (participants in each group) performed three tasks measuring different aspects of inhibition: stop-signal performance (active inhibition), inhibition of return (reflexive inhibition), and attentional blink (temporal inhibition). The comparison examined group differences in reaction times and facilitation/inhibition patterns. Analysis tested which aspects of inhibition differed between groups.

what_manipulated: semantic alignment of prime words with arithmetic problem structure (aligned vs misaligned relations)
what_measured: automatic activation of arithmetic fact solutions (measured via priming of subsequent arithmetic tasks)
experiment: Units (digit pairs in addition tasks) were primed by word pairs denoting semantic relationships that either aligned (categorical) or misaligned (functional, unrelated) with addition structure. The comparison examined whether obligatory activation of arithmetic facts occurred only for aligned primes. Analysis used priming paradigm to test automatic analogical coordination.

what_manipulated: sequence repetition throughout session (repeated vs novel sequences in serial recall)
what_measured: learning of both correct responses and error patterns in repeated sequences
experiment: Units (response productions in recall) were tracked over repeated presentations of the same sequence. The comparison was between sequences that appeared multiple times versus novel sequences. Analysis examined learning trajectories based on both correct prior responses and incorrect prior responses, measuring whether response probability increased with prior occurrence.

what_manipulated: stimulus quality (clarity) and word frequency, crossed with nonword type (legal vs pseudohomophone)
what_measured: reaction time distributions in a word-nonword discrimination task
experiment: Units (response times across many trials) were analyzed for effects of stimulus quality and frequency under two different nonword conditions. The comparison examined mean reaction times and distributional shapes for fast and slow responses. Analysis revealed whether effects were additive in means but interactive in distributional tails, suggesting different processing routes.

what_manipulated: timing of distractor word presentation relative to picture (simultaneous vs delayed by 1000ms) and semantic relatedness
what_measured: latency of picture naming response
experiment: Units (naming responses) were measured under conditions where distractor words appeared either immediately or after a delay. The comparison examined semantic interference effects (target vs related distractors) and word frequency effects across timing conditions. Analysis tested whether postlexical semantic interference differed from lexical-level effects based on response timing.

what_manipulated: covariance structure of visual features (colors and shapes paired consistently, partially, or uncoupled) during implicit learning
what_measured: accuracy of detecting learned sequences when features are tested individually vs as bound objects
experiment: Units (sequence judgments in a test phase) were obtained after learning displays with different feature covariance patterns. The comparison examined whether observers showed sequence learning for bound feature pairs, individual features, or both depending on training covariance. Analysis tested whether statistical learning operates on objects or features based on their correlation structure.

what_manipulated: frequency of outcomes under single-cause and dual-cause conditions in contingency judgment tasks
what_measured: causal rating assigned to candidate causes
experiment: Units (causal judgments) were collected for two-cause scenarios with varied outcome frequencies and contingency structures. The comparison examined how judgments of one cause were reduced by increasing outcomes of the other cause alone. Analysis tested a weighted averaging model accounting for how well each cause explains outcome occurrences versus raw contingency relationships.

what_manipulated: task instructions (recall order vs recall timing) and list structure (grouped vs ungrouped presentation)
what_measured: accuracy of serial recall and timing reproduction for irregularly spaced digit sequences
experiment: Units (recalled items and inter-item intervals) were obtained under different instructional sets and temporal structures. The comparison examined whether grouping instructions similarly affected order and timing recall, and whether actual timing of list presentation predicted both types of recall. Analysis examined the roles of timing signals versus positional representations in serial order memory.

what_manipulated: task demands requiring active processing of adjacent versus nonadjacent element relations in learning materials
what_measured: accuracy of recognizing or producing learned relations between elements
experiment: Units (learned dependencies) were extracted from materials embedding both adjacent and nonadjacent relations. The comparison examined whether participants learned only the type of relation their task required them to process actively, regardless of spatial contiguity. Analysis tested sufficiency of joint attentional processing for learning, versus necessity of contiguity.

what_manipulated: presence and timing of retro-cue directing attention to one item after encoding but before test
what_measured: change detection accuracy and estimates of working memory capacity
experiment: Units (change detection responses) were obtained with and without retro-cues delivered 1 second after encoding and 0.5 seconds before testing. The comparison examined whether attention-directing cues improved detection performance beyond explanations of memory decay or interference reduction. Analysis tested mechanisms of working memory resistance to interference.

what_manipulated: stochastic environment structure (different task types with different outcome distributions)
what_measured: risk-taking choices and learning rates across sequential decision opportunities
experiment: Units (risky choices in sequential decision tasks) were made in environments with different learning requirements and outcome distributions. The comparison examined whether Bayesian learning processes and decision processes varied across task types. Analysis examined interference of learning with risk assessment and diagnostic validity for identifying real-world risk-takers.

what_manipulated: presence of moral nonconformity (participant or observed other) and whether observer was involved in the morally questionable act
what_measured: liking ratings and attributions about target person
experiment: Units (participant ratings) were collected for targets exhibiting moral nonconformity versus conformity under conditions where raters were either involved or uninvolved in the moral decision. The comparison examined whether involvement in the act determined rejection of nonconformists. Analysis tested mediation by perception of implicit reproach and buffering by prior value affirmation.

what_manipulated: structural similarity between implicit and explicit attitude test formats
what_measured: correlation between implicit and explicit attitude measurements (racial attitudes)
experiment: Units (paired attitude assessments) were collected using implicit tests (affect misattribution procedure) and explicit tests that varied in their structural resemblance. The comparison examined implicit-explicit correlations as task format similarity increased. Analysis tested whether weak correlations in prior research reflected independent cognitive processes versus task-dependent differences.

what_manipulated: age (10 to 20 years old) with measurement of personality via self-report Big Five inventory
what_measured: psychometric properties: acquiescence, factor coherence, cross-domain differentiation, and item comprehension difficulty
experiment: Units (personality self-reports from an internet sample) were analyzed at different age levels. The comparison examined year-by-year developmental trends in response patterns and factor structure. Analysis examined whether gains in coherence and differentiation across domains varied by personality domain.

what_manipulated: mortality salience activation and individual differences in attachment style
what_measured: death-thought accessibility, worldview defense, self-worth, memory bias, attraction to similar others, and reliance on attachment figures
experiment: Units (responses to mortality salience induction) were measured across six experiments varying mortality salience conditions and assessing attachment style. The comparison examined effects of mortality salience on psychological outcomes and whether secure versus insecure individuals differed in terror management strategies (parents vs romantic partners). Analysis tested attachment theory mechanisms in existential threat responses.

what_manipulated: activation of general action versus inaction goals via word primes (action, rest) in seven experiments
what_measured: magnitude of motor output (doodling, eating) and cognitive output (recall, problem-solving)
experiment: Units (output behaviors across diverse tasks) were measured after priming. The comparison examined whether the same primes influenced both motor and cognitive output similarly. Analysis tested whether diverse behavioral effects resulted from instigation of general action goals versus inaction goals by examining whether attaining the desired state diminished prime effects.
